---
title: "Practice Repositories"
---

Before working in the full project, we use two smaller repositories to check the technical basics.

The goal is to separate setup problems from application design problems:

1. First, confirm that Python can connect to SQL Server.
2. Then, confirm that Streamlit can display SQL query results in the browser.
3. After that, move to the full application.

:::{.callout-note}
The full SOPRA project uses `APP_USER` for audit fields when records are created or updated. The two practice repositories only run read queries, so they do not need `APP_USER` for their SQL logic. It is still useful to fill it consistently in `.env` when you prepare for the CRUD project.
:::

## Repository 1: SQL Server Connection

Use this repository first:

- [Open mssql-example on GitHub](https://github.com/kirenz/mssql-example)
- [Download mssql-example as ZIP](https://github.com/kirenz/mssql-example/archive/refs/heads/main.zip)

With Git, use the following commands in your terminal (one at a time):

```bash
git clone https://github.com/kirenz/mssql-example.git
cd mssql-example
uv sync
cp .env.example .env
uv run python sql_example_sopra.py
```

This repository is intentionally small. It focuses on:

- loading database credentials from `.env`
- building a SQLAlchemy connection
- running a simple SQL query against the SOPRA views
- reading the result into a `pandas` DataFrame

The SOPRA example script reads the discount overview:

```sql
SELECT TOP (5)
    RabattID,
    Kunde,
    MengeVon,
    MengeBis,
    RabattProzent,
    GiltVon,
    GiltBis
FROM list_views.V_LIST_B2B_DISCOUNT
ORDER BY RabattID DESC;
```

It also reads the customer list of values used later by the Streamlit dropdown:

```sql
SELECT TOP (5)
    CUSTOMER_ID,
    CUSTOMER_LONG
FROM dbo.LOV_CUSTOMER
ORDER BY CUSTOMER_ID;
```

If this step fails, fix the driver, credentials, network access, or SOPRA database permissions before continuing.

## Repository 2: Streamlit and SQL Server

Use this repository after the plain SQL connection works:

- [Open streamlit-mssql on GitHub](https://github.com/kirenz/streamlit-mssql)
- [Download streamlit-mssql as ZIP](https://github.com/kirenz/streamlit-mssql/archive/refs/heads/main.zip)

With Git:

```bash
git clone https://github.com/kirenz/streamlit-mssql.git
cd streamlit-mssql
uv sync
cp .env.example .env
uv run streamlit run app.py
```

This repository adds the browser interface. It focuses on:

- starting a Streamlit app
- connecting Streamlit to SQL Server
- entering and running a SQL query against the SOPRA views
- displaying the result as a table

If this step works, the basic Streamlit and SQL Server setup is ready.

## Repository 3: Full CRUD Application

Use the main repository after the two practice steps:

- [Open sopra on GitHub](https://github.com/kirenz/sopra)
- [Download sopra as ZIP](https://github.com/kirenz/sopra/archive/refs/heads/main.zip)

With Git:

```bash
git clone https://github.com/kirenz/sopra.git
cd sopra
uv sync
cp .env.example .env
uv run python sql_example.py
uv run streamlit run app.py
```

The main project introduces the application architecture:

- `app.py` handles the Streamlit user interface
- `services/` contains business rules and validation
- `repositories/` contains SQL Server access
- `models/` contains domain data structures

:::{.callout-note}
Each repository has its own `.env` file. Credentials stay local on your machine and are not committed to GitHub.
:::
